data.title: Noem faces mounting criticism from Republicans, Democrats after Minneapolis killing
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem is coming under intense pressure over her handling of the shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, raising questions about her future atop the department.

President Trump made multiple moves on Monday in response to the outcry over the shooting. Chief among them were the installation of border czar Tom Homan atop the efforts in the Twin Cities and phone calls with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) in a bid to lower the temperature.

He also met for two hours on Monday night with Noem, who has become the face of an immigration crackdown that is no longer popular with Americans and who has seen her limited political cache zapped among lawmakers on both sides of the aisle. 

“Those two people told the president before they even had an incident report whatsoever that the person who died was a terrorist. That is amateur hour at its worst,” Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) said about Noem and Stephen Miller, a top aide to the president, before focusing on Noem.

“What she’s done in Minnesota should be disqualifying. She should be out of a job,” he told reporters. “It’s just amateur-ish. It’s terrible. It’s making the president look bad on policy that he won on. He won on a strong message on immigration. Now, nobody’s talking about that. … They’re talking about the incompetence of the leader of the Homeland Security [Department].”

Tillis went on to pan Noem for being among those responsible for “squandering the opportunity” to codify positive parts of the administration’s immigration efforts.

He also heaped criticism on Gregory Bovino, the since-removed Border Patrol commander in Minneapolis, saying he “needs to go back to California, get his retirement papers and go.”

“They got to de-escalate there and treat these communities with some respect,” he added. 

Lawmakers on both sides of the aisle have expressed dismay with Noem’s comments immediately after a Border Patrol agent fatally shot Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse at a local Veterans Affairs hospital, as she compared him to a “domestic terrorist.” 

“[Pretti] had a weapon on him, and multiple — dozens — of rounds of ammunition; wishing to inflict harm on these officers, coming, brandishing like that,” she said. Pretti had a valid concealed carry permit and there is no evidence Pretti brandished a gun, which multiple videos show an agent removing from his waistband seconds before he was shot. 

“Violence against a government because of ideological reasons and for reasons to resist and to perpetuate violence. That is the definition of domestic terrorism,” Noem added. 

Senate Republicans other than Tillis also indicated they are frustrated at the missteps, with some members arguing that she is hemorrhaging support from the conference. 

One GOP lawmaker labeled the comments an “unforced error.”

“You don’t get a second chance to make a first impression,” the Senate Republican said. “You don’t get a chance to unring a bell.”

Underscoring that dwindling level of support were numerous public remarks calling for increased scrutiny of the shooting. 

Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), the chairman of the Senate Homeland Security Committee, called for the heads of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to testify before his panel amid the rising furor. That is set to take place on Feb. 12 — two days after they appear before the House Homeland Security panel. 

News also emerged on Monday that Noem is expected to testify before the Senate Judiciary Committee on March 3 — an appearance that lawmakers have long sought but was hung up for numerous reasons, including a negotiation over the amount of time she would testify for. Lewandowski was a key figure in that effort. 

Her response prompted an uptick in support for articles of impeachment against her among House Democrats. As of Tuesday afternoon, more than 160 had signed on to support it. 

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) also threw his weight behind that effort on Tuesday, saying Trump should fire her and that otherwise the formal impeachment effort will move forward. 

“We can do this the easy way or the hard way,” he and his fellow Democratic leaders said. 

Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.), who has sided with Republicans on key votes, on Tuesday lobbed a “direct appeal to immediately fire” the DHS secretary. 

“Americans have died. She is betraying DHS’s core mission and trashing your border security legacy. DO NOT make the mistake President Biden made for not firing a grossly incompetent DHS Secretary,” Fetterman wrote on X, referring to former Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas. 

Those comments also got her in hot water with GOP members not known for lobbing on-the-record criticism.

Sen. John Curtis (R-Utah) called for those involved in the shooting to be “held accountable,” before going after the DHS secretary. 

“Officials who rush to judgment before all the facts are known undermine public trust and the law-enforcement mission,” he wrote on X. “I disagree with Secretary Noem’s premature DHS response, which came before all the facts were known and weakened confidence.”

The incident and the aftermath have also created problems in the effort to fund the government as Senate Democrats zero in on taking down the DHS spending bill. 

Rep. Mark Amodei (R-Nev.), a top negotiator of that measure, said that Noem “didn’t do herself any favors when she called this guy a domestic terrorist.

“Sending Tom Homan to check in on one of Kristi Noem’s departments operating up there after the last few weeks is not a sign of confidence,” he told the Nevada Independent.

The right-wing media ecosystem even got into the mix to dig at Noem. The cover of Tuesday’s New York Post, an outlet owned by Rupert Murdoch, mockingly referred to the DHS secretary as “ICED BARBIE” — a spin on her nickname often used in left-wing circles. 

Nevertheless, Trump gave her another sign of support on Tuesday, a day after White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said that “he still has confidence and trust” in the secretary. 

“I am yeah. I think she’s doing a very good job,” he said before flying out for an event in Iowa, arguing that the work at the border has been a success

 Asked for comment, DHS pointed The Hill to Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin’s comments on “America’s Newsroom” Tuesday morning.

 McLaughlin said Trump and Noem had “at-length discussions” but declined to go into details.

“Secretary Noem will continue to oversee the Department of Homeland Security, and she is very happy that Tom Homan, who is a great asset to the President, has a great depth of experience and insight, will be overseeing Minneapolis,” McLoughlin said.

Top Republicans noticeably did not go as far as Trump to defend Noem. When asked if he has confidence in her, Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) demurred.

“The important thing is that the president’s got confidence in his team,” he said. 

Julia Manchester contributed.

data.title: Tillis says Noem should be 'out of a job' over Minneapolis response
data.description:
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) on Monday called for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to be ousted from her job after she labeled a slain protester a “domestic terrorist” without evidence over the weekend.

Tillis became the first Senate Republican to call for Noem to be fired in the aftermath of the death of Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse at a Minneapolis-area Veterans Affairs hospital, who was shot multiple times by a Border Patrol agent. 

“What she’s done in Minnesota should be disqualifying. She should be out of a job,” he said. “It’s just amateur-ish. It’s terrible. It’s making the president look bad on policy that he won on. [President Trump] won on a strong message on immigration. Now, nobody’s talking about that. … They’re talking about the incompetence of the leader of the Homeland Security [Department].”

“Those two people told the president before they even had an incident report whatsoever that the person who died was a terrorist. That is amateur hour at its worst,” Tillis said about Noem and Stephen Miller, a top aide to the president.

Tillis went on to pan Noem for being among those responsible for “squandering the opportunity” to codify positive parts of the immigration efforts.

He also heaped criticism on Gregory Bovino, the since-removed Border Patrol commander at-large, saying he “needs to go back to California, get his retirement papers and go.”

“They got to de-escalate there and treat these communities with some respect,” he added. 

The North Carolina Republican has been critical of Noem in recent months for her refusal to testify before the Judiciary Committee. She is expected to do that on March 3.

One rubicon Tillis declined to cross was impeachment, saying that it should instead be “a management decision.”

“She needs to go,” he said. 

More than 1460 House Democrats have signed on to articles of impeachment against her, and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) backed that effort on Tuesday.

data.title: Ben & Jerry's co-founder: 'ICE must be defunded and disbanded'
data.description:
Ben & Jerry’s co-founder Ben Cohen on Tuesday called for Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to be “defunded and disbanded” after a U.S. Border Patrol agent shot and killed a man in Minneapolis on Saturday, the second deadly shooting involving federal law enforcement this month.

Cohen wrote on the social platform X that he had planned to create an ice cream honoring 37-year-old Renee Good, who was fatally shot by an ICE officer on Jan. 7, but the killing of Alex Pretti, also 37, over the weekend stopped him.

“I was prepared to make a special ice cream today to memorialize and celebrate the life of Renee Good, but now that Alex’s murder makes it clear that the murder of Renee and the government’s lies were not a mistake but standard operating procedure, I just don’t have it within me,” Cohen said in an accompanying video.

Cohen used strong language to describe how he views the Trump administration’s actions and goals.

“We all live in Minneapolis now, because Minneapolis is the only the beginning of what they have in mind. They’re coming for anyone, anywhere who doesn’t submit,” he said, likening ICE to a “brazen, arrogant, masked militarized force loyal only to Trump and immune from prosecution.”

“Submit or be murdered. Video them and be murdered. Protest and be murdered or at least be placed on a list of domestic terrorists and investigated,” he said, adding, “This is not freedom.”

“This is not the right to free speech or the right to protest. This is not America. This is sheer cruelty. This is the beginning of the end of the land of the free, unless we make it the home of the brave, unless we’re brave enough to stand up for justice, to stand up for our neighbors, to stand up for compassion,” he continued.

Cohen further called for the agency to be dissolved, advocating for a return to the Immigration and Naturalization Service, which handled immigration issues before the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks.

“Let’s go back to that,” Cohen said.

Magnum Ice Cream Company, Ben & Jerry’s parent company, did not immediately respond to The Hill’s request seeking comment on Cohen’s remarks.

Calls to “abolish ICE” have grown louder in recent weeks as the Trump administration faces mounting pressure to scale back its immigration enforcement operations, specifically in Minnesota, amid ongoing intense clashes between protestors and federal law enforcement agents.

The heads of ICE, Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) have been summoned to testify before the Senate Homeland Security Committee next month.

More than half of Americans said in an Economist/YouGov poll taken in the days before and after Pretti’s death that they have “very little” confidence in ICE.

A separate New York Times/Siena poll found that nearly two-thirds of registered voters surveyed disapprove of how ICE is handling its job, while 36 percent said they approve of the agency’s work.

data.title: GOP confidence in ICE declines in January: Poll
data.description:
Fewer Republicans say in a new poll that they have confidence in U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), the agency tasked with carrying out the Trump administration’s crackdown on illegal immigration.

In the Economist/YouGov survey, conducted this past weekend, 60 percent of Republicans say they trust ICE, including 31 percent who say they have a “great deal” of confidence in the institution and 29 percent who say they have “quite a lot” of confidence in the institution.

That marks a statistically significant drop from the 68 percent of Republicans who said two weeks ago that they have confidence in ICE and the 69 percent who said the same a month before that.

The latest survey shows the share of Republicans who have “very little” confidence in ICE rose to 16 percent this week, up from 8 percent two weeks ago and 9 percent in mid-December.

The decline in GOP voters’ confidence in ICE mirrors a tonal shift among Republican officials, who have shown an increasing willingness to express concern about ICE enforcement tactics after two officer-involved shootings in Minneapolis left much of the country on edge. Since respondents were asked about their confidence in the agency in mid-December, an ICE officer fatally shot 37-year-old Renee Good following a Jan. 7 confrontation while she was in her vehicle.

Trump, too, has seemed to pick up on voter concerns about the aggressive federal response to anti-immigration demonstrations, distancing himself from members of his administration who jumped to the defense of the Border Patrol agent who fatally shot another 37-year-old Minneapolis resident, Alex Pretti, on Saturday.

Three-quarters of respondents took the survey after the Pretti shooting.

The survey also shows an increase in the share of independent voters who say they have “very little” confidence in ICE, jumping to 67 percent this week, up from 54 percent two weeks ago and 49 percent in mid-December.

Overall, 55 percent of Americans say they have “very little” confidence in ICE, up from 49 percent two weeks ago and 45 percent last month.

The survey, conducted Jan. 23-26, included 1,684 citizens and had a margin of error of 3.3 percentage points.

data.title: Frey to Homan: Minneapolis 'will not enforce federal immigration laws'
data.description:
Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) on Tuesday said he spoke with border czar Tom Homan and reinforced that the city would not change its sanctuary policies in exchange for the departure of federal immigration authorities.

“I shared with Mr. Homan the serious negative impacts this operation has had on Minneapolis and surrounding communities, as well as the strain it has placed on our local police officers,” Frey wrote in a post on the social media platform X.

“I also made it clear that Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws, and that we will remain focused on keeping our neighbors and streets safe,” he added.  

Frey said that city leaders will continue to stay in conversation with Homan and his team. Gov. Tim Walz (D-Minn.) met with the border czar for a “productive” conversation earlier in the day after two U.S. citizens were killed by immigration authorities in Minneapolis. 

Trump administration officials, including Homan and Attorney General Pam Bondi, have urged local officials to comply with requests from Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) to help detain migrants without legal status in the area. 

During a Sunday press conference, Walz told reporters that the state continues to comply with obligations mandated by federal law, including turning over inmates who are determined to be residing in the country illegally. 

“They’re taking credit for people that we’ve had in jail for a long time,” Walz said, referencing ICE touting the number of detainments in the state. 

“We always hand them over,” he added.

The Minnesota governor, who chose to bypass a reelection bid amid a statewide fraud scandal, urged Bondi to focus on releasing the Jeffrey Epstein files instead of local immigration operations.

“I would just give a pro tip to the Attorney General, there’s two million documents in the Epstein files we’re still waiting on. Go ahead and work on those,” he said during the press conference.

data.title: Yale announces free tuition for families making less than $200,000
data.description:
Yale University announced Tuesday that families with an income below $200,000 will get free tuition, following other highly ranked schools who have unveiled generous financial packages in recent years. 

The change, which will go into effect the 2026-2027 academic year, will give families making less than $200,000 scholarships to cover tuition — and those whose incomes are less than $100,000 will have all costs eliminated, including housing and meal plan. 

“I am thrilled that Yale is making this important investment in affordability,” said Dean of Undergraduate Admissions and Financial Aid Jeremiah Quinlan. “With this announcement, we reiterate and reinforce Yale’s commitment to ensuring that cost will never be a barrier between promising students and a Yale College education.”  

The announcement says 50 percent of American households with school-aged chidlren would be eligible for a zero-cost education at Yale. 

Currently, 56 percent of the undergraduate class at Yale receive need-based aid and 1,000 students qualify for a no-cost degree. 

Yale currently has a $90,000 a year sticker price.  

“Yale makes a special effort to support students with the greatest financial need,” said Kari DiFonzo, director of undergraduate financial aid. “Beyond generous financial aid offers, Yale provides extra grants for students to purchase winter clothing, to pursue summer experiences abroad, and to respond to unexpected financial hardships.”  

The announcement follows other prestigious universities such a Harvard, the University of Pennsylvania and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology announcing more generous financial aid packages for lower income students.

data.title: ICE agent charged with misdemeanor after altercation with activist in Illinois
data.description:
An Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer is facing a misdemeanor charge after an alleged altercation with an activist in Illinois last month, according to local police.

Brookfield Police Department Chief Michael Kuruvilla said in a statement that officers responded to a gas station on Dec. 27 to investigate an alleged battery.

The victim, Chicago-based attorney Robert Held, told the Riverside-Brookfield Landmark that he had been filming the officer, Adam Saracco, with his cellphone from the sidewalk when Saracco approached him.

“He put the gas hose down, and he started briskly walking towards me. When he got closer, he broke into a run, and I was stunned. He put his hands on me. He threw me to the ground and aggressively was on top of me, trying to grab my phone,” Held, 68, told the outlet.

Held posted footage of the alleged incident, spliced with other clips, on Instagram the day after. The video has since garnered roughly 100,000 views and been shared over 1,700 times.

“This is about our First Amendment right to film what government agents do in our communities. If they can attack one person with a camera, they can scare everyone else into looking away. Don’t look away,” Held said over the video.

The New York Times reported that Held was not injured and that Saracco was off-duty at the time of the incident, having just left a nearby immigration detention center.

Kuruvilla said in the statement that the Cook County State’s Attorney’s Office reviewed the case and declined to file felony charges, advising that it would be more appropriately charged as a misdemeanor.

Saracco cooperated with investigators and was subsequently “charged, cited, and released on one count of misdemeanor battery with an assigned court date,” Kuruvilla added.

A spokesperson for the Department of Homeland Security called it “gross” that Saracco was facing charges in a statement to The Hill on Tuesday.

“It is gross that our officer is facing charges for responding to a direct threat against him and his safety. We stand firmly behind our officer. We won’t accept this stay tuned,” the statement continued.  

The spokesperson claimed Saracco had been “targeted and aggressively harassed by a known ICE agitator,” noting that Held had previously been arrested outside an ICE facility in Broadview.

“While off duty and driving home in his personal vehicle, the officer was stalked by this individual to a local gas station. The agitator confronted the officer, filmed him at close range, and recorded the officers personal license plate,” the statement continued, describing Held’s actions as “clear attempts to dox” the officer.

“The officer, who was alone and without protective equipment, acted to protect himself when faced with this threatening behavior,” the spokesperson added.

Held told the Landmark he was “pleased” Saracco would face some accountability.

“They did a thorough investigation. They conducted themselves professionally, and I am pleased that justice will be done,” he said.

As the Trump administration continues to send federal law enforcement officers to U.S. cities as part of a massive immigration crackdown, Democratic leaders have encouraged residents to document their interactions.

“People of Illinois, we need your help. Get your cell phones out – record what you see. Put it on social media,” Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D) wrote last September on the social platform X.  

“Peacefully ask for badge numbers and identification. Speak up for your neighbors. We need to let the world know this is happening – and that we won’t stand for it,” Pritzker added.

data.title: Live updates: Trump speaks in Iowa as he defends Noem from resignation calls
data.description:
President Trump is speaking in Des Moines, Iowa, as he defends Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem from criticism of her handling of the weekend killing of a Minneapolis man by a Border Patrol (CBP) agent.

The shooting ignited a firestorm on both sides of the aisle, with Democrats calling for Noem to be fired or impeached, and Republican lawmakers expressing grave concern and calling for hearings.

Trump on Tuesday, when asked if Noem will step down, told reporters, “No.”

“I think she’s doing a very good job. The border is totally secure. You know, you forget we had a border that I inherited where millions of people were coming through. Now we have a border where no one is coming through. They come into our country only legally,” Trump added.

The Senate, meanwhile, is back in Washington on Tuesday as a partial government shutdown looms. But Democrats are eyeing funding for Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) has indicated he intends to move forward with a six-bill funding package, which includes DHS, despite Democrats’ threats to block the measure. Thune must confront whether he can rally his conference and the White House around a modified bill that separates DHS funding from the package to avoid another shutdown.

It also comes as Trump is making adjustments to his signature initiatives in the wake of Alex Pretti’s death on Saturday. Border czar Tom Homan will arrive Tuesday in Minneapolis, replacing Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino.

Earlier Tuesday, a federal judge also demanded that acting Immigration and Customs Enforcement chief Todd Lyons make an appearance in court later this week, threatening the law enforcement officer with contempt proceedings.

Follow live below.

data.title: More than half of Americans have 'very little' confidence in ICE: Poll
data.description:
More than half of Americans have “very little” confidence in Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), according to a poll taken in the days before and after Alex Pretti was shot and killed by a federal officer in Minneapolis.

In The Economist/YouGov poll, 55 percent of respondents said they had “very little” confidence in ICE, while 16 percent said they have “some” confidence in the agency. Sixteen percent said they have “quite a lot” of confidence in ICE and 14 percent said they have “a great deal.”

The poll, conducted between Jan. 23 and 26, comes amid rising anger directed at the Trump administration over its immigration agenda and actions in Minneapolis, specifically.

Earlier this month, an ICE officer fatally shot Renee Good, a mother of three, as she attempted to drive away. On Saturday, a Border Patrol agent shot and killed Pretti, an intensive care nurse for veterans.

A growing chorus of Republicans have called for an independent investigation into Pretti’s killing and de-escalation in Minnesota. Leaders of Trump’s immigration agencies have also been called to testify in the GOP-controlled Senate.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) on Tuesday warned of leading impeachment proceedings targeting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, whose department houses ICE and Border Patrol, if President Trump does not remove her from her role.

“Donald Trump must fire Kristi Noem immediately,” Jeffries said on the social platform X. “Or Democrats will initiate impeachment proceedings against her in the House. We can do this the easy way or the hard way.”

The Economist/YouGov poll took place from Jan. 23 to 26, featuring 1,684 people with a 3.3 percentage point margin of error.

data.title: Trump on Bovino: 'A pretty out there kind of guy'
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday referred to Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino as “a pretty out there kind of guy” when asked if his decision to have Bovino leave Minneapolis was a “pull back.” 

Trump said in some cases being “out there” was “good” and then suggested that may have not been the case in Minnesota in the aftermath of outrage erupting over the shooting death of U.S Citizen and ICU nurse Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents.

“I don’t think it’s a pullback. It’s a little bit of a change. Everyone in this room that has a business, you know you make little changes,” Trump told Fox News’ Will Cain during a swing through Iowa. 

“You know, Bovino’s very good but he’s a pretty out there kind of a guy, and in some cases that’s good, maybe it wasn’t good here,” he continued. 

Bovino had tangled with both Democrats and Republicans on social media as videos circulated of Pretti’s shooting. Some lawmakers called for a full investigation into the matter as multiple videos from various angles showed Pretti being tackled and then shot in the back.

Bovino told CNN on Sunday that Pretti was attempting to “massacre” law enforcement, but video of the incident shows the ICU nurse filming agents and attempting to assist a woman they had pushed to the ground before he was surrounded, disarmed and shot.

Other Trump officials also took to social media to give their assessment of Pretti’s killing.

White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller referred to Pretti as a “would-be assassin” who “tried to murder federal law enforcement” in a post on X that was retweeted by Vice President JD Vance. 

On Monday, White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt tried to distance Trump from such remarks, saying the president was looking for a full review of the incident.

When asked in Iowa about such remarks by his administration, Trump said he “hadn’t heard that.”
